active.end: Unclear / original version discontinued, later revival appears absent from mainstream retail

hook: A retro grape theater-box candy with a long, confusing history: original Ferrara-era product, later discontinued, later revived, and now hard to find again.
fate: Alexander the Grape was introduced by Ferrara in 1971, discontinued in its original form in the 1980s, and later brought back under 1908 Candy. Today it appears difficult to find in mainstream retail, with sold-out retailer pages and no strong official product page controlling the query.
summary: Alexander the Grape is a grape-flavored retro candy that began under Ferrara in 1971. Candy-history sources say the original version was discontinued in the 1980s, then later reintroduced under 1908 Candy. That makes the brand more complicated than a simple one-time discontinuation story. The best current description is that Alexander the Grape is a revived legacy candy with weak modern retail visibility and fragmented information online.

overview_md:
**Alexander the Grape** is one of those candy brands that looks simple at first but gets more complicated the more you dig into it.

The clearest candy-history sources say **Ferrara introduced Alexander the Grape in 1971**. They also say the original version was **discontinued in the 1980s**. If that were the whole story, this would be a straightforward discontinued-candy brand.

But that is not the full picture. Later retailer and candy-history pages say the brand was **brought back under 1908 Candy**. At the same time, current retail signals are weak. **Sweeties** shows it as a **discontinued item** and sold out, while **Candy Favorites** also has sold-out or discontinued listings.

That is why the safest current description is not simply 'active' or 'gone forever.' A better summary is that **the original candy was discontinued, the brand later had a revival, and current mainstream availability still appears weak**.

That in-between status is what makes Alexander the Grape a useful content target. The search results are fragmented: sold-out retailer pages, candy-history references, and nostalgia posts, but no strong official page that clearly explains the full story.

The most useful content angle is not just 'Is Alexander the Grape discontinued?' It is the fuller question: **what happened to the original candy, what came back later, and can you still buy it today?**

faq.question: Is Alexander the Grape discontinued?
faq.answer: The original Ferrara-era version was discontinued in the 1980s. The brand was later revived under 1908 Candy, but current retailer pages suggest it is still hard to find and may again be absent from mainstream retail.

faq.question: When did Alexander the Grape come out?
faq.answer: Candy Favorites says Ferrara introduced Alexander the Grape in 1971.

faq.question: Did Alexander the Grape come back?
faq.answer: Yes. Candy Favorites says the brand was reintroduced by 1908 Candy in 2020.

faq.question: Why is Alexander the Grape a good fragmented-SERP topic?
faq.answer: Because the current results are scattered across sold-out retailer pages, candy-history references, and nostalgia posts, with no strong official explainer controlling the query.

faq.question: What is the best content angle for Alexander the Grape?
faq.answer: The strongest angle is: what happened to Alexander the Grape, when the original was discontinued, what came back later, and whether you can still buy it today.
